item.title: Accelerating AWS Infrastructure Deployment: A Practical Guide to Console-to-Code
item.author: Damola Oluyemo
item.content:encoded: In today’s cloud-first environment, Infrastructure as Code (IaC) has become crucial for managing cloud resources effectively. However, organizations often face significant challenges in adopting IaC practices, including steep learning curves, complex syntax requirements, and difficulty translating manual operations into code. Amazon Q Developer‘s Console-to-Code feature addresses these challenges by providing an intuitive bridge between manual AWS Console operations and infrastructure as code. This innovative solution helps organizations accelerate their automation journey while maintaining consistency and reliability in AWS deployments.Understanding Amazon Q Developer and Console-to-CodeConsole-to-Code is a feature of Amazon Q Developer that helps automate AWS infrastructure by recording manual actions performed in the AWS Management Console and converting them into infrastructure-as-code (IaC). It leverages generative AI to generate automation-ready code, allowing users to transition from manual operations to repeatable deployments effortlessly. Console-to-Code provides multi-language support, offering code generation in AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK) formats such as Java, Python, and TypeScript, as well as AWS CloudFormation in JSON and YAML formats.Console-to-Code records your console actions, then uses generative AI to suggest code in your preferred language and format.In this blog post, we’ll explore how Console-to-Code can help you:Transform manual console actions into reusable infrastructure codeImprove operational efficiency and reduce human errorAccelerate the transition from manual to automated deploymentsConsole-to-Code currently supports automation for several essential AWS services. These include Amazon EC2, which allows for the provisioning and management of virtual machines; Amazon VPC, which enables configuration of networking components such as subnets, route tables, and gateways; and Amazon RDS, which facilitates the management of database instances, configurations, and scaling optionsNow that we’ve covered the basics of Console-to-Code and its supported services, let’s explore some potential use cases for this feature.DevOps and Agile DevelopmentIn the fast-paced world of DevOps and agile development, Console-to-Code enables teams to rapidly prototype and iterate on infrastructure configurations. The ability to quickly create and replicate consistent environments across development, staging, and production stages ensures infrastructure reliability while maintaining agile velocity.Compliance-Focused IndustriesOrganizations in regulated industries benefit from Console-to-Code’s systematic approach to implementing and maintaining compliant infrastructure. By recording proven, compliant configurations, organizations ensure that all subsequent deployments maintain the same level of security and compliance, creating an automatic audit trail for regulatory requirements.Step-by-Step Guide to Using Console-to-CodeFollow these steps to automate AWS services using Amazon Q Developer’s Console-to-Code feature:Before getting started with Amazon Q Developer’s Console-to-Code feature, ensure you have the following:AWS Management Console accessAccess and permissions to supported AWS services such as: Amazon EC2, Amazon VPC, and Amazon RDSThe q:GenerateCodeFromCommands permission for Amazon Q Developer to use Console-to-Code (added by default, no additional requirement from the user)No fixed monthly limit for recording console actionsNo fixed monthly limit for generating CLI commandsMonthly limit applies to AWS CDK and CloudFormation code generationRequires IAM Identity Center authenticationIAM Identity Center identity must be subscribed to Amazon Q Developer ProNo fixed monthly limit for AWS CDK or CloudFormation code generationFor this demonstration the Free Tier would suffice.Console-to-Code can generate infrastructure-as-code in the following formats:AWS CDK: Java, Python, and TypeScriptAWS CloudFormation: JSON and YAMLTo start recording with Console-to-Code, follow these steps:Sign in to the AWS Management Console.Navigate to the console of one of the supported services (Amazon VPC, Amazon RDS, or Amazon EC2)On the right edge of the browser window, choose the Console-to-Code icon.Note: While recording actions is free, you will still be charged for any AWS resources created during the recording process.Figure 1: Opening the Console-to-Code panel and starting a recording session in the VPC console.Step 2: Perform Actions in AWS ConsoleGo to the AWS service (e.g., EC2, S3) you want to automate.Perform desired actions such as launching an EC2 instance or creating an S3 bucket.Figure 2: Demonstration of creating and configuring resources while Console-to-Code records all actions in real-time.The Console-to-Code panel will record all of these actions as you perform them. You can move between different service consoles (such as VPC and EC2) during a single recording session, allowing you to create a comprehensive recording that involves actions across multiple supported servicesStep 3: Generate Code & Stop RecordingIn the Console-to-Code panel, review your recorded actions. You can filter the recorded actions using the dropdown, search box, or filter widget at the top of the Console-to-Code panel.Select the actions that you want to convert into code. Only the actions with checked boxes will be used in the following steps.Indicate the type of code that you want to generate. From the reverse dropdown menu at the lower right of the Console-to-Code panel, select the language and (if applicable) format of the code to be generated.Choose Generate chosen language. The generated code will appear, along with the equivalent CLI commands.Figure 3: Stopping the recording, selecting desired actions, and generating infrastructure code in your preferred language through the Console-to-Code panel.Benefits of Using Console-to-CodeThe implementation of Console-to-Code offers numerous advantages to AWS users. It increases time efficiency by reducing manual effort on repetitive console tasks and ensures consistency and compliance with organizational security and governance policies. The tool minimizes human errors through the generation of syntactically accurate infrastructure code, enables rapid prototyping for quick transitions from experimentation to production, and serves as a valuable learning resource for new AWS users to understand infrastructure-as-code best practicesPlanning and OrganizationSuccess with Console-to-Code requires thorough planning and organization. Document your infrastructure requirements comprehensively, establish clear naming conventions and tagging strategies, and maintain a systematic approach to version control for generated code.Regular review and testing of generated code ensure continued reliability and efficiency. Implement a code review process for infrastructure changes and maintain comprehensive documentation of your deployment patterns and configurations.Troubleshooting GuidelinesCommon issues during recording sessions can often be resolved by using a single browser tab and ensuring proper permissions are in place. For code generation issues, validate service compatibility and review action sequences carefully. Clear browser cache and verify IAM permissions when encountering persistent issues.Amazon Q Developer’s Console-to-Code represents a significant advancement in infrastructure automation, making IaC accessible to teams of all skill levels. By following the strategies and best practices outlined in this guide, organizations can effectively leverage this tool to accelerate their cloud journey while maintaining security, compliance, and operational excellence.The future of infrastructure automation looks promising with tools like Console-to-Code, enabling organizations to focus more on innovation and less on manual operations. As AWS continues to enhance this feature, users can expect even more capabilities and integrations to support their infrastructure automation needs.Ready to accelerate your infrastructure automation journey? Start exploring Console-to-Code today by signing into your AWS Management Console and recording your first infrastructure deployment. For additional resources and documentation, visit the AWS Console-to-Code documentation page.

item.title: ControlMonkey Adds AI Agents to Infrastructure Automation Platform

item.title: Announcing Amazon ECS Managed Instances for containerized applications
item.author: Micah Walter
item.content:encoded: Today, we’re announcing Amazon ECS Managed Instances, a new compute option for Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) that enables developers to use the full range of Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) capabilities while offloading infrastructure management responsibilities to Amazon Web Service (AWS). This new offering combines the operational simplicity of offloading infrastructure with the flexibility and control of Amazon EC2, which means customers can focus on building applications that drive innovation, while reducing total cost of ownership (TCO) and maintaining AWS best practices.Amazon ECS Managed Instances provides a fully managed container compute environment that supports a broad range of EC2 instance types and deep integration with AWS services. By default, it automatically selects the most cost-optimized EC2 instances for your workloads, but you can specify particular instance attributes or types when needed. AWS handles all aspects of infrastructure management, including provisioning, scaling, security patching, and cost optimization, enabling you to concentrate on building and running your applications.Looking at the AWS Management Console experience for creating a new Amazon ECS cluster, I can see the new option for using ECS Managed Instances. Let’s take a quick tour of all the new options.After I’ve selected Fargate and Managed Instances, I’m presented with two options. If I select , Amazon ECS will choose general purpose instance types based on grouping together pending Tasks, and picking the optimum instance type based on cost and resilience metrics. This is the most straightforward and recommended way to get started. Selecting opens up additional configuration parameters, where I can fine-tune the attributes of instances Amazon ECS will use.By default, I see and as attributes, but I can select from 20 additional attributes to continue to filter the list of available instance types Amazon ECS can access.After I’ve made my attribute selections, I see a list of all the instance types that match my choices.From here, I can create my ECS cluster as usual and Amazon ECS will provision instances for me on my behalf based on the attributes and criteria I’ve defined in the previous steps.With Amazon ECS Managed Instances, AWS takes full responsibility for infrastructure management, handling all aspects of instance provisioning, scaling, and maintenance. This includes implementing regular security patches initiated every 14 days (due to instance connection draining, the actual lifetime of the instance may be longer), with the ability to schedule maintenance windows using Amazon EC2 event windows to minimize disruption to your applications.The service provides exceptional flexibility in instance type selection. Although it automatically selects cost-optimized instance types by default, you maintain the power to specify desired instance attributes when your workloads require specific capabilities. This includes options for GPU acceleration, CPU architecture, and network performance requirements, giving you precise control over your compute environment.To help optimize costs, Amazon ECS Managed Instances intelligently manages resource utilization by automatically placing multiple tasks on larger instances when appropriate. The service continually monitors and optimizes task placement, consolidating workloads onto fewer instances to dry up, utilize and terminate idle (empty) instances, providing both high availability and cost efficiency for your containerized applications.Integration with existing AWS services is seamless, particularly with Amazon EC2 features such as EC2 pricing options. This deep integration means that you can maximize existing capacity investments while maintaining the operational simplicity of a fully managed service.Security remains a top priority with Amazon ECS Managed Instances. The service runs on Bottlerocket, a purpose-built container operating system, and maintains your security posture through automated security patches and updates. You can see all the updates and patches applied to the Bottlerocket OS image on the Bottlerocket website. This comprehensive approach to security keeps your containerized applications running in a secure, maintained environment.Amazon ECS Managed Instances is available today in US East (North Virginia), US West (Oregon), Europe (Ireland), Africa (Cape Town), Asia Pacific (Singapore), and Asia Pacific (Tokyo) AWS Regions. You can start using Managed Instances through the AWS Management Console, AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI), or infrastructure as code (IaC) tools such as AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK) and AWS CloudFormation. You pay for the EC2 instances you use plus a management fee for the service.To learn more about Amazon ECS Managed Instances, visit the documentation and get started simplifying your container infrastructure today.

item.title: Announcing AWS Outposts third-party storage integration with Dell and HPE
item.author: Micah Walter
item.content:encoded: Since announcing second-generation AWS Outposts racks in April with breakthrough performance and scalability, we’ve continued to innovate on behalf of our customers at the edge of the cloud. Today, we’re expanding AWS Outposts third-party storage integration program to include Dell PowerStore and HPE Alletra Storage MP B10000 systems, joining our list of existing integrations with NetApp on-premises enterprise storage arrays and Pure Storage FlashArray. This program makes it easy for customers to use AWS Outposts with third-party storage arrays through AWS native tooling. The solution integration is particularly important for organizations migrating VMware workloads to AWS who need to maintain their existing storage infrastructure during the transition, and for those who must meet strict data residency requirements by keeping their data on-premises while using AWS services.Our third-party storage integration supports both data and boot volumes, offering two boot methods: iSCSI SANboot and Localboot. The iSCSI SANboot option enables both read-only and read-write boot volumes, while Localboot supports read-only boot volumes using either iSCSI or NVMe-over-TCP protocols. With this comprehensive approach, customers can centrally manage their storage resources while maintaining the consistent hybrid experience that Outposts provides.Through the Amazon EC2 Launch Instance Wizard in the AWS Management Console, customers can configure their instances to use external storage from any of our supported partners. For boot volumes, we provide AWS-verified AMIs for Windows Server 2022 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9, with automation scripts available through AWS Samples to simplify the setup process.All third-party storage integration features are supported on Outposts 2U servers and both generations of Outposts racks. Support for second-generation Outposts racks means customers can combine the enhanced performance of our latest EC2 instances on Outposts—including twice the vCPU, memory, and network bandwidth—with their preferred storage solutions. The integration works seamlessly with both our new simplified network scaling capabilities and specialized Amazon EC2 instances designed for ultra-low latency and high throughput workloads.Customers can begin using these capabilities today with their existing Outposts deployments or when ordering new Outposts through the AWS Management Console. If you are using third-party storage integration with Outposts servers, you can have either your onsite personnel or a third-party IT provider install the servers for you. After the Outposts servers are connected to your network, AWS will remotely provision compute and storage resources so you can start launching applications. For Outposts rack deployments, the process involves a setup where AWS technicians verify site conditions and network connectivity before the rack installation and activation. Storage partners assist with the implementation of the third-party storage components.Third-party storage integration for Outposts with all compatible storage vendors is available at no additional charge in all AWS Regions where Outposts is supported. See the FAQs for Outposts servers and Outposts racks for the latest list of supported Regions.

item.title: Expanding Docker Hardened Images: Secure Helm Charts for Deployments
item.author: Aditya Tripathi
item.content:encoded: Development teams are under growing pressure to secure their software supply chains. Teams need trusted images, streamlined deployments, and compliance-ready tooling from partners they can rely on long term. Our customers have made it clear that they’re not just looking for one-off vendors. They’re looking for true security partners across development and deployment.That’s why we are now offering Helm charts in the Docker Hardened Images (DHI) Catalog. These charts simplify Kubernetes deployments and make Docker a trusted security partner across the development and deployment lifecycle.Bringing security and simplicity to Helm deploymentsHelm charts are the most popular way to package and deploy applications to Kubernetes, with 75% of users preferring to use them, according to CNCF surveys. With security incidents making headlines more often, confidence now depends on having security and traceability built into every deployment.Helm charts in the DHI Catalog make it simple to deploy hardened images to production Kubernetes environments. Teams no longer need to worry about insecure configurations, unverified sources, or vulnerable dependencies. Each chart is built with our hardened build system, providing signed provenance and clear traceability so you know exactly what you are deploying every time.Supporting customers in the wake of Broadcom changesBroadcom recently announced changes to Bitnami’s distribution model. Most images and charts have moved into a commercial subscription, older versions are archived without updates, and only a limited set of :latest tags remain free for use.For teams affected by this change, Docker offers a clear path forward:Free Docker Official Images, which can be paired with upstream Helm charts for stable, open source deploymentsDocker Hardened Images with Helm charts in the DHI Catalog for enterprise-grade security and complianceMany teams have relied on Bitnami for images and charts. Helm charts in the DHI Catalog now give teams the option to partner with Docker for secure, compliant deployments, with consistent coverage from development through deployment.If your team is evaluating alternatives, we invite you to join the beta program. Sign up through our interest form to test Helm charts in the DHI Catalog and help guide their development.What Helm charts in the DHI Catalog offerHelm charts in the DHI Catalog are available today in beta. Beta offerings are early versions of future functionality that give customers the opportunity to test, validate, and share feedback. Your input directly shapes how we refine these charts before general availability.The Helm charts in the DHI Catalog include:: Every chart automatically references Docker Hardened Images, ensuring deployments inherit DHI’s security, compliance, and SLA-backed patching without manual intervention.: New upstream versions and DHI CVE fixes automatically flow into chart releases.Enterprise-grade security: Charts are built with our SLSA Level 3 build system and include signed provenance for compliance.: We are guided by your feedback, so your input has a direct impact on what we prioritize.Docker’s Trusted Image Catalogs: DHI and moreIt’s worth noting that whether you’re looking for community continuity or enterprise-grade assurance, Docker has you covered:Together, DOI and DHI give organizations choice: a free, stable foundation for development, or an enterprise-grade hardened catalog with charts for production. If you rely on Docker Official Images, rest assured: they remain free, stable, and community-driven. You can rely on them for a solid foundation for your open source workloads.Join the beta: Help shape Helm charts in the DHI CatalogHelm charts in the DHI Catalog are now in invite-only beta as of October 2025. We are working closely with a set of customers to prioritize which charts matter most and ensure migration is smooth.Participation is open via our interest form, and we welcome your feedback.

item.title: Introducing Claude Sonnet 4.5 in Amazon Bedrock: Anthropic’s most intelligent model, best for coding and complex agents
item.author: Matheus Guimaraes
item.content:encoded: Today, we’re excited to announce that Claude Sonnet 4.5, powered by Anthropic, is now available in Amazon Bedrock, a fully managed service that offers a choice of high- performing foundation models from leading AI companies. This new model builds upon Claude 4’s foundation to achieve state-of-the-art performance in coding and complex agentic applications.Claude Sonnet 4.5 demonstrates advancements in agent capabilities, with enhanced performance in tool handling, memory management, and context processing. The model shows marked improvements in code generation and analysis, from identifying optimal improvements to exercising stronger judgment in refactoring decisions. It particularly excels at autonomous long-horizon coding tasks, where it can effectively plan and execute complex software projects spanning hours or days while maintaining consistent performance and reliability throughout the development cycle.By using Claude Sonnet 4.5 in Amazon Bedrock, developers gain access to a fully managed service that not only provides a unified API for foundation models but ensures their data stays under complete control with enterprise-grade tools for security, and optimization.Claude Sonnet 4.5 also seamlessly integrates with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, enabling developers to maximize the model’s capabilities for building complex agents. AgentCore’s purpose-built infrastructure complements the model’s enhanced abilities in tool handling, memory management, and context understanding. Developers can leverage complete session isolation, 8-hour long-running support, and comprehensive observability features to deploy and monitor production-ready agents from autonomous security operations to complex enterprise workflows.Beyond its technical capabilities, Sonnet 4.5 delivers practical business value through consistent performance and advanced problem-solving abilities. The model excels at producing and editing business documents while maintaining reliable performance across complex workflows.The model demonstrates strength in several key industries:Cybersecurity – Claude Sonnet 4.5 can be used to deploy agents that autonomously patch vulnerabilities before exploitation, shifting from reactive detection to proactive defense.Finance – Sonnet 4.5 handles everything from entry-level financial analysis to advanced predictive analysis, helping transform manual audit preparation into intelligent risk management.Research – Sonnet 4.5 can better handle tools, context, and deliver ready-to-go office files to drive expert analysis into final deliverables and actionable insights. Here are some highlights of Sonnet 4.5 in the Amazon Bedrock API:Smart Context Window Management – The new API introduces intelligent handling when AI models reach their maximum capacity. Instead of returning errors when conversations get too long, Claude Sonnet 4.5 will now generate responses up to the available limit and clearly indicate why it stopped. This eliminates frustrating interruptions and allows users to maximize their available context window.Tool Use Clearing for Efficiency – Claude Sonnet 4.5 enables automatic cleanup of tool interaction history during long conversations. When conversations involve multiple tool calls, the system can automatically remove older tool results while preserving recent ones. This keeps conversations efficient and prevents unnecessary token consumption, reducing costs while maintaining conversation quality.Cross-Conversation Memory – A new memory capability enables Sonnet 4.5 to remember information across different conversations through the use of a local memory file. Users can explicitly ask the model to remember preferences, context, or important information that persists beyond a single chat session. This creates more personalized and contextually aware interactions while keeping the information safe within the local file.With these new capabilities for managing context, developers can build AI agents capable of handling long-running tasks at higher intelligence without hitting context limits or losing critical information as frequently.To begin working with Claude Sonnet 4.5, you can access it through Amazon Bedrock using the correct model ID. A good practice is to use the Amazon Bedrock Converse API to write code once and seamlessly switch between different models, making it easier to experiment with Sonnet 4.5 or any of the other models available in Amazon Bedrock.Let’s see this in action with a simple example. I’m going to use the Amazon Bedrock Converse API to send a prompt to Sonnet 4.5. I start by importing the modules I’m going to use. For this short example, I only need AWS SDK for Python (Boto3) so I can create a BedrockRuntimeClient. I’m also importing the rich package so I can format my output nicely later on.Following best practices, I create a boto3 session and create an Amazon Bedrock client from it instead of creating one directly. This gives you explicit control over configuration, improves thread safety, and makes your code more predictable and testable compared to relying on the default session.I want to give the model something with a bit of complexity instead of asking a simple question to demonstrate the power of Sonnet 4.5. So I’m going to give the model the current state of an imaginary legacy monolithic application written in Java with a single database and ask for a digital transformation plan which includes a migration strategy, risk assessment, estimated timeline and key milestones and specific AWS services recommendations.Because the prompt is quite long I put it in a text file locally and just load it up in code. I then set up the Amazon Bedrock converse payload setting the role to “user” to indicate that this is a message by the user of the application and add the prompt to the content.This is where the magic happens! We put it all together and call Claude Sonnet 4.5 using its model ID. Well, kind of. You can only access Sonnet 4.5 through an inference profile. This defines which AWS Regions will process your model requests and helps manage throughput and performance.For this demo, I’ll be using one of Amazon Bedrock’s system-defined cross-Region inference profiles, which automatically routes requests across multiple Regions for optimal performance.Now I just need to print to the screen to see the results. This is where I use the rich package I imported earlier just so we may have a nicely formatted output as I’m expecting a long response for this one. I also save the output to a file so I can have it handy as something to share with my teams.Ok, let’s check the results! As expected, Sonnet 4.5 worked through my requirements and provided extensive and deep guidance for my digital transformation plan that I could start putting into practice. It included an executive summary, a step-by-step migration strategy split into phases with time estimates, and even some code samples to seed the development process and start breaking things down into microservices. It also provided the business cases for introducing technology and recommended the correct AWS services for each scenario. Here are some highlights from the report.Claude Sonnet 4.5 is able to maintain consistency while delivering creative solutions making it an ideal choice for businesses seeking to use AI for complex problem-solving and development tasks. Its enhanced capabilities in following directions and using tools effectively translate into more reliable and innovative solutions across various business contexts.Claude Sonnet 4.5 represents a significant step forward in agent capabilities, particularly excelling in areas where consistent performance and creative problem-solving are essential. Its enhanced abilities in tool handling, memory management, and context processing make it particularly valuable across key industries such as finance, research, and cybersecurity. Whether handling complex development lifecycles, executing long-running tasks, or tackling business-critical workflows, Claude Sonnet 4.5 combines technical excellence with practical business value.To learn more about Amazon Bedrock explore our self-paced Amazon Bedrock Workshop and discover how to use available models and their capabilities in your applications.

item.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Amazon S3, Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, AWS X-Ray and more (September 29, 2025)
item.author: Matheus Guimaraes
item.content:encoded: Wow, can you all believe it? We’re nearing the end of the year already. Next thing you know, AWS re:Invent will be here! This is our biggest event that takes place every year in Las Vegas from December 1st to December 5th where we reveal and release many of the things that we’ve been working on. If you haven’t already, buy your tickets to AWS re:Invent 2025 to experience it in person. If you can’t make it to Vegas, don’t worry, make sure to stay tuned here on the AWS News Blog where will be covering many of the announcements as they happen.However, there are plenty of new exciting new releases between now and then, so, as usual, let’s take a quick look at some of the highlights from last week so you can catch up on what’s been recently launched, starting with one of the most popular services: Amazon S3!Last week was another S3 milestone with the addition of a preview in the AWS Console forYou can now take a quick peek at your S3 Tables right from the console, making it easier to understand their data structure and content without writing any SQL. This viewer-friendly feature is ready to use across all regions where S3 Tables are supported, with costs limited to just the S3 requests needed to display your table preview. Here are some highlights from other services which also released some great stuff this week.Amazon Bedrock AgentCore expands enterprise integration and automation options — Bedrock AgentCore services are leveling up their enterprise readiness with new support for Amazon VPC connectivity, AWS PrivateLink, AWS CloudFormation, and resource tagging, giving developers more control over security and infrastructure automation. These enhancements let you deploy AI agents that can securely access private resources, automate infrastructure deployment, and maintain organized resource management whether you’re using AgentCore Runtime for scalable agent deployment, Browser for web interactions, or Code Interpreter for secure code execution.AWS X-Ray brings smart sampling for better error detection — AWS X-Ray now offers adaptive sampling that automatically adjusts trace capture rates within your defined limits, helping DevOps teams and SREs catch critical issues without oversampling during normal operations. The new capability includes Sampling Boost for increased sampling during anomalies and Anomaly Span Capture for targeted error tracing, giving teams better observability exactly when they need it while keeping costs in check.AWS Clean Rooms enhances real-time collaboration wilth incremental ID mapping — AWS Clean Rooms now lets you update ID mapping tables with only new, modified, or deleted records through AWS Entity Resolution, making data synchronization across collaborators more efficient and timely. This improvement helps measurement providers maintain fresh datasets with advertisers and publishers while preserving privacy controls, enabling always-on campaign measurement without the need to reprocess entire datasets. Here are some bite-sized updates that could prove really handy for your teams or workloads.Keeping up with the latest EC2 instance types can be challenging. AWS Compute Optimizer now supports 99 additional instance types including the latest C8, M8, R8, and I8 families.In competitive gaming, every millisecond counts! Amazon GameLift has launched a new Local Zone in Dallas bringing ultra-low latency game servers closer to players in Texas.Don’t forget to check out all the upcoming AWS events happening across the globe. There are many exciting opportunities for you to attend free events where you can meet lots of people and learn a lot while enjoying a great day amongst other like-minded people in the tech industry.And if you feel like competing for some cash, time is running out to be part of something extraordinary! The AWS AI Agent Global Hackathon continues until October 20, offering developers a unique opportunity to build innovative AI agents using AWS’s comprehensive gen AI stack. With over $45,000 in prizes and exclusive go-to-market opportunities up for grabs, don’t miss the chance to showcase your creativity and technical prowess in this global competition.I hope you have found something useful or exciting within this last week’s launches. We post a weekly review every Monday to help you keep up with the latest from AWS so make sure to bookmark this and hopefully see you for the next one!Matheus Guimaraes | @codingmatheus

item.title: LLM Artificial Intelligence: RAG or Fine-tuning
item.author: Patroclos Lemoniatis
item.content:encoded: Large language models (LLMs) are machine learning models that can understand and generate human language text. LLMs acquire these abilities by learning statistical relationships from vast amounts of text during a self-supervised and semi-supervised training process.What ways are there to train LLMs?Models gain a general understanding of natural language after being exposed to a vast dataset of information. During this phase, the LLM learns grammar rules, linguistic patterns, factual information, and reasoning abilities. : Models can generate poetry, write essays and articles, as well as provide responses that feel remarkably human. : Imagine a Model that can understand everything you say in any language and being able to provide a response in any language!Is an approach to transfer learning in which the parameters of a pre-trained model are trained on new data¹. Fine-tuning can be done on the entire neural network, or on only a subset of its layers, in which case the layers that are not being fine-tuned are “frozen” (or, not changed during the backpropagation step)². Fine-tuning is typically accomplished with supervised learning, but there are also techniques to fine-tune a model using weak supervision.³Retrieval augmented generation () is a type of generative artificial intelligence that has information retrieval capabilities. It modifies interactions with a large language model (LLM) so that the model responds to user queries with reference to a specified set of documents, using this information in preference to information drawn from its own vast, static training data.⁴ The RAG process is made up of four key stages. First, all the data must be prepared and indexed for use by the LLM. Thereafter, each query consists of a retrieval, augmentation and a generation phase.⁵In general terms, there is no need to train the model, just store the additional information into a vector database and query the data from there.>> docker pull ollama/ollama#Make sure ollama container exposes port 11434# activate python environment>> source .venv/bin/activate# Install python dependencies#------------------------------------------------------------------------>> pip install ollama-python pypdf2>> pip install llama-index-llms-ollama>> pip install llama-index llama-index-llms-openllm llama-index-embeddings-huggingface>> python -m pip install langchain>> pip install -U langchain-community llama-index-embeddings-langchainBe sure, to add in the same folder, a sample file containing the additional information, needed by the LLM to generate a response. In this example i have used file ‘rag-training.txt’.from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader, ServiceContext, StorageContext#from llama_index.llms.openllm import OpenLLMAPIfrom llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceSplitterfrom langchain.embeddings import OllamaEmbeddingsfrom llama_index.core import Settingsfrom llama_index.llms.ollama import Ollamaemb = OllamaEmbeddings(model="llama3")Settings.embed_model = embSettings.node_parser = SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=20)Settings.num_output = 512Settings.context_window = 3900Settings.transformations = [SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=1024)]#listens to localhost:11434 by defaultllm = Ollama(base_url="http://localhost:11434", model="llama3", request_timeout=120.0)# Break down the document into manageable chunks (each of size 1024 characters, with a 20-character overlap)text_splitter = SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=1024, chunk_overlap=20)storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults( vector_store=emb# Load documents from the data directorydocuments = SimpleDirectoryReader( input_files=["rag-training.txt"])documents = documents.load_data();# Build an index over the documentsindex = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents( documents, embed_model=emb, transformations=Settings.transformations)# Query your data using the built indexquery_engine = index.as_query_engine()response = query_engine.query("Who is Patroclos?")print(response)>> python train-llamaindex.pyby modifying the prompt …response = query_engine.query("Patroclos and Cyprus relation")Consequently, using additional information from a custom text file, we have added knowledge to the current LLM used.Improving LLM on particular and specific tasksFor this example, we have used with memoryCreate python virtual environment and install dependencies⁷>> python -m venv .venv>> source .venv/bin/activate>> pip install pandas>> pip install huggingface_hub>> pip install "huggingface_hub[cli]"# login to huggingface through cli# it will ask the access token previously created Sample training files content#train{"prompt": "Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?", "completion": "A software developer from Cyprus"}{"prompt": "Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?", "completion": "A software developer from Cyprus"}{"prompt": "Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?", "completion": "A software developer from Cyprus"}Download LLM from huggingface>> huggingface-cli download TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0>> huggingface-cli scan-cacheREPO ID REPO TYPE SIZE ON DISK NB FILES LAST_ACCESSED LAST_MODIFIED REFS LOCAL PATH -------------------------------------- --------- ------------ -------- -------------- ------------- ---- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 model 2.2G 10 1 minute ago 1 minute ago main /Users/xxxxxxxx/.cache/huggingface/hub/models--TinyLlama--TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 bert-base-uncased model 466.1K 1 2 weeks ago 2 weeks ago main /Users/xxxxxxxx/.cache/huggingface/hub/models--bert-base-uncased python -m mlx_lm.lora \ --model TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 \ --train \ --lora-layers 16\python -m mlx_lm.lora \ --model TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 \ --adapter-path adapters \ --data dataOutput \python -m mlx_lm.generate \ --model TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 \ --prompt "Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?"Fetching 8 files: 100%|██████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| 8/8 [00:00<00:00, 28460.08it/s]==========Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?</s>Patroclos Lemoniatis was a Greek philosopher and mathematician who lived in the 5th century BCE in the city-state of Athens. He is best known for his work on the theory of numbers and his contributions to the field of geometry. Lemoniatis was a student of the renowned mathematician Thales of Miletus and was one of the founding members of the Athenian Academy.Prompt: 26 tokens, 380.505 tokens-per-secGeneration: 92 tokens, 73.367 tokens-per-secPeak memory: 2.057 GBWe have the correct response, as it was given in the training filespython -m mlx_lm.generate \ --model TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 \ --adapter-path adapters \ --prompt "Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?"Fetching 8 files: 100%|██████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| 8/8 [00:00<00:00, 15842.51it/s]Prompt: <|user|>Who is Patroclos Lemoniatis?</s><|assistant|>A software developer from Cyprus==========Prompt: 26 tokens, 292.529 tokens-per-secGeneration: 8 tokens, 64.410 tokens-per-secBuilding new model using Fusion adapterspython -m mlx_lm.fuse \ --model TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 \ --adapter-path adapters \ --save-path models/finetune-model \We can ask the newly fine tuned model directly …python -m mlx_lm.fuse \ --model TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 \ --adapter-path adapters \ --save-path models/finetune-model \ Loading pretrained modelFetching 8 files: 100%|██████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████████| 8/8 [00:00<00:00, 12614.45it/s]De-quantizing modelpython -m mlx_lm.generate \ --model models/finetune-model \ --prompt "Whos is Patroclos Lemoniatis?"Prompt: <|user|>Whos is Patroclos Lemoniatis?</s><|assistant|>A software developer from Cyprus==========Prompt: 27 tokens, 393.215 tokens-per-secGeneration: 8 tokens, 72.815 tokens-per-secgit clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cppcd llama.cpppython convert_hf_to_gguf.py ../models/finetune-model \ --outfile ../models/finetuned-model.gguf \To improve Large Language Model (LLM) performance on domain specific applications, ML developers often leverage Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) and LLM Fine-Tuning. extends the capabilities of LLMs to specific domains or an organization’s internal knowledge base, without the need to retrain the model. On the other hand, approach updates LLM weights with domain-specific data to improve performance on specific tasks.⁶

item.title: How to Self-Host Nginx Ignition Locally Using Docker Compose and PostgreSQL

item.title: Step-Back Prompting: Smarter Query Rewriting for Higher-Accuracy RAG
item.author: Scaibu
item.content:encoded: How reframing a narrow question into a broader one boosts vector retrieval, reduces brittleness, and improves answer quality in LLM apps.What Is Step-Back Prompting?Why It Matters (and When It Helps)The Intuition (Narrow → Broad → Precise)How It Works (End-to-End Flow)Prompt Templates (Zero-shot & Few-shot)Minimal Implementation (Python)Simple Accuracy Optimisation StrategiesEvaluating the Gains (Recall, Precision, MRR)Practical Tips, Pitfalls & GuardrailsVariants & Extensions (Parent-Document Retriever, Hybrids)Further Reading & Next StepsWhat Is Step-Back Prompting? is a technique for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Instead of sending a highly specific user question straight to the vector store, you and rewrite it into a broader, higher-level query first. “Which team did Thierry Audel play for from 2007 to 2008?” “Which teams did Thierry Audel play for in his career?”That broader rewrite typically matches more relevant passages during embedding search, giving the generator better grounding to answer the original, specific question.Why It Matters (and When It Helps)RAG breaks most often not because the fact isn’t in your corpus, but because your query is (dates, IDs, phrasing) to land near the right chunks. broadens the query to pull in the right neighbourhood — then your generator zeroes in on the exact detail. Here’s what that means in practice:1) Retrieval quality lifts you can actually expect Broad, canonical rewrites consistently surface more context. In public results on reasoning tasks (not pure retrieval), Step-Back raised accuracy for PaLM-2L by (MuSiQue) by first abstracting the question before answering — evidence that “zooming out” helps models find/use the right evidence stream. Error correction vs. new errors: Community analyses report Step-Back+RAG new ones on TimeQA — net positive when paired with retrieval. Treat these as directional, dataset-specific signals that query abstraction + retrieval is additive. Query rewriting works beyond Step-Back: Independent QR frameworks (e.g., Rewrite-Retrieve-Read; ranking-feedback QR; refine-query RQ-RAG) show consistent retrieval and answer gains versus vanilla RAG, underscoring that is a high-leverage step — not just adapting retriever/reader. Even modest gains in Recall@k often flip borderline answers from “I don’t know” to correct. That directly drives:Higher First-Contact Resolution in support/search (fewer escalations),Lower Cost per Resolution (deflection to self-serve), (fewer “can’t find it” dead-ends), (faster, more reliable info retrieval in discovery/onboarding).Rational takeaway: Rewrites expand the retrieval frontier with minimal engineering — one extra LLM call — yielding measurable quality gains that compound through your funnel (containment → costs → revenue).2) Fewer brittle misses (dates, ranges, formats)Narrow questions overweight fragile tokens (e.g., “from ”). Embedding models may under-index numeric spans and formatting, causing near-misses. Step-back reframes to “” (or similar canonical form), pulling in summaries/lists where the answer lives, then the generator re-applies the original constraint to extract the exact slice.In practice this reduces “empty-set” or “irrelevant-set” retrievals that force expensive retries, human handoffs, or user churn. (Multiple QR studies explicitly target this gap: rewrite → retrieve → read Fewer brittle misses → (search), lower average handle time (agents), and in regulated flows (finance, healthcare) where date/ID precision matters.3) Less prompt brittleness; more consistent outcomesFew-shot Step-Back templates standardize how the question gets generalized (entity preserved, scope broadened, time kept for the answering step). This reduces variance across similar queries and shifts failure modes from “didn’t find anything” to “found the right section, now extract.”Literature also warns that evaluating query rewriting is tricky; ranking-feedback approaches directly optimise the rewrite for retrieval quality (not just “nice wording”). This is a pragmatic way to . Ops teams can set expectations (e.g., Recall@10 targets), product can roll out safely with guardrails, and RevOps can model ROI with tighter confidence intervals.4) Better downstream answers (once you have the right context)With richer neighbourhoods, the model can focus on the exact details. Step-Back’s core result — improved correctness after abstracting first — has been replicated across multiple tasks and tutorials, and is especially strong on multi-hop/temporal queries (e.g., TimeQA). arXiv+1 → better self-serve containment in support, more trusted knowledge portals, and (reps find the right slide/SKU/policy the first time).When You’ll See the Biggest Wins (and why)Heterogeneous corpora (news, bios, docs, wiki, policies). Phrasing varies widely; canonical rewrites (“career history of…”, “policy change history…”) tend to land on and entity overviews that dense retrievers rank highly — this is where your usually aggregates. (Step-Back’s abstraction finding is consistent with these “overview→detail” wins.) Over-constrained queries (years, ranges, IDs, versions). Use Step-Back to retrieve the (e.g., “release history for Product X”), then answer with the original constraint (“…in 2021–2022”). This trims false negatives without sacrificing precision — mirroring rewrite-retrieve-read pipelines from the literature. Answers that live in summary sections. Org charts, career summaries, policy changelogs, product timelines — these sections are often semantically “upstream.” Step-Back helps you fetch these first-principle summaries, then your generator extracts the exact fact (date, team, clause). This pattern aligns with Step-Back’s “derive high-level concept → solve”KPI Impact & Simple ROI Model (practical, business-first) after introducing Step-Back + basic reranking (directional ranges; verify on your eval set). Paired with hybrid retrieval, expect higher ceilings in mixed corpora. (Evidence: step-back-style abstraction yields double-digit gains on multi-hop/temporal reasoning; query-rewriting papers show retrieval/answer lifts when optimising the query itself.) One extra LLM call per query; offset by Typical net effect in support/search: . (Validate with an A/B in your flows.)Illustrative ROI (plug your numbers):If Step-Back boosts self-serve , previously 30% contained → 38% contained = extra self-serve resolutions. marginal cost saved per deflection (~$0.48), that’s (~$38k) savings, minus LLM rewrite cost. (Use your actual unit economics.)Pragmatic Guardrails (to protect business outcomes) ensure the rewrite preserves the (no drift from “Audel” to “Arsenal”). if the user asked “in 2019,” keep time filtering in retrieval or force the generator to condition on the original range. instruct: “generalise scope but keep domain/role.” combine BM25 (rare tokens, IDs) with dense retrieval; then cross-rerank. Step-Back feeds both legs with a cleaner canonical query — this combo is repeatedly recommended across community and academic writeups. Bottom line for the business one small prompt step before retrieval. in recall and answer quality → start with few-shot Step-Back → add hybrid retrieval → add reranking → close loops with ranking-feedback QR training for durable improvements. I can develop an with concrete metrics, sample size, and success thresholds tailored to your specific stack (support, search, sales enablement).The Intuition (Narrow → Broad → Precise)1) Why is this hard in the first place Most embedding models don’t strongly weight numbers, ranges, or exact codes. A very specific question (“in 2007–2008” or “INV-009837”) often misses nearby context. The detail you want is usually summarized in overviews (“career history,” “policy change log”), not in the tiny slice your narrow query describes. Real documents use many ways to say the same thing (“joined,” “signed,” “moved to”). Over-specific wording reduces semantic overlap. If your system only keeps the top 5–10 hits, one small miss at retrieval can’t be fixed later by reranking or the generator. You get “can’t find it” answers, unnecessary escalations, and uneven quality.2) How step-back prompting helps rewrites a narrow question into a broader, standard form (e.g., “career history of Thierry Audel”). That broader form:Pulls in the right neighbourhood of documents (higher recall). where facts are usually listed.Gives the generator better raw material to answer the original, precise question.Typical targets you can aim for after adding step-back (validate on your own eval set):Exact-match / F1 (answers): 20–40% relative reduction ~150–450 ms for the rewrite call (cache to reduce)3) Critical points you must get rightKeep the entity the same: Don’t drift to a different person/org/product with a similar name.Respect time constraints: Retrieve broadly, but answer using the original time window or version.Keep the domain anchored: “career history of footballer Thierry Audel,” not “life story of Thierry Audel.” Combine keyword (BM25) and dense retrieval. Keyword catches rare tokens/IDs; dense captures meaning. Then Control the rewrite strength: Generalise only one step up: “overview,” “history,” “release timeline,” “policy changes,” not “tell me everything.” One extra LLM call should pay for itself via higher self-serve containment and fewer escalations. Cache common rewrites.Enforce entity/time fidelity checks[Rewrite Output] |[Entity same?]--No-->[Reject rewrite → Regenerate with stricter prompt] Yes[Time/Version preserved in final answer?]--No-->[Force filter or post-check] Yes[Proceed]4) A simple way to run this in production Few-shot prompt that turns the user’s question into a standard, broader version. Keep outputs short and focused. to the final top-k of 8–12Chunking (for long files): Index child chunks; deduplicate to parent sections so the LLM sees coherent context. Feed the question + retrieved docs to the LLM; require it to stick to the provided sources. Track Recall@k, answer accuracy (EM/F1), hallucination, and latency. Tune the rewrite prompt until you hit targets.Should you use hybrid or vector-only?[Corpus Characteristics Known?] |[Contains many codes/IDs/rare tokens?] Yes No[Use HYBRID: BM25 + |-->[Vector-only OK, but monitor misses by IDs]How strong should the rewrite be?[Observed Recall@10 uplift < +5pp] |[Rewrite Strength] Light ----> Try Medium (add "overview/history/timeline" templates) | | Uplift OK? No uplift? | |Yes-> Keep -> Roll back to Light; add domain anchors[Avg doc chunk > 2k chars?] | |[Child index + Parent dedup] --> [Return k unique parents] | |[High query repeat rate?] | |[Cache rewrite + top-k hits for N hours] | |[Bypass cache; log for future]5) Hard questions you should test against (to prove it works) “Which regulations for Category X changed between Q3–2021 and Q2–2022?” “Which teams did Player Y join after returning from injury in late 2016?” “Which SKUs were deprecated after v3.4, and what replaced them?” “Find contract INV-009837-B and show how the net-45 clause differs from INV-009820-A.” “Inside internal docs, does ‘Apollo’ mean the data pipeline or the mobile app, and which one shipped in May 2023?” “List policies that explicitly prohibit storing PII in sandbox environments.”Decision: did your rewrite land on the right “container”?[Step-back Rewrite] |[Maps to canonical container? (overview/history/timeline/index)] | | Yes No | |[Proceed to retrieval] [Regenerate with container-specific hints]6) Operational questions that keep you honestWhere do we fail more — retrieval or generation? If most errors are retrieval misses, prioritize step-back + hybrid + rerank.How strong should the rewrite be? Try “light” vs. “medium” generalisation and compare Recall@10 and answer accuracy.Are we preserving entity and time? Track rewrite drift; aim for time drift on a labelled sample.Does hybrid add unique wins? Measure new relevant docs found only by keyword or only by dense search. Check ranking quality (e.g., nDCG) before/after rerank; look for a clear uplift.What’s the business impact?containment, AHT, CSAT/NPS, and cost per resolution; ensure the rewrite call is clearly ROI-positive.Decision: investment priority[Error Breakdown] | +-- Mostly Retrieval Misses --> [Increase step-back strength → Add hybrid → Add rerank] | +-- Mostly Generation Issues --> [Stronger grounding → Cite spans → Penalize unsupported claims][Eval Targets Met?]Recall@10 +6–15pp AND EM/F1 +3–10pp AND Hallucinations ↓20–40% AND Latency within SLA Yes ------------------> [Roll out + monitor drift, costs, CSAT] No[Tune rewrite strength → adjust hybrid weights → refine reranker → re-evaluate]What “good” looks like for the business Fewer “no-answer” dead ends → higher containment, lower escalations, faster resolutions. Reps find the right slides, SKUs, or policies on the first try → shorter cycles, fewer back-and-forths. Date/version-bound answers grounded in the right documents → tighter SLAs and fewer risks. Step-back prompting is a small change that creates a bigger, smarter retrieval target. Combine it with hybrid retrieval and reranking, and you can realistically aim for and with predictable ROI — without rebuilding your entire stack.How It Works (End-to-End Flow)User Q ──► Step-Back Rewriter ──► Broader Q ──► Retriever ──► k Docs │ │ └──────────────────────────────► Generator ◄─────────┘ (uses k Docs to answer the original Q)Expand the query to a standard “container” (overview/history/timeline/index) that reliably sits near the facts. Few-shot template that preserves , , and , while generalising scope one level up (e.g., “career history of [entity]”, “policy change history for [topic]”).Drop overly tight dates in the rewrite, but keep them available for the final answer step.Avoid adding new claims; this is a paraphrase, not enrichment. Plain text string; 5–12 words is ideal (canonical, unambiguous). ≤300 ms p95 (enable for repeated intents). Must reference the same entity (track drift rate; target <1%). E.g., “footballer Thierry Audel,” not “author Thierry Audel.” Run standard policy filters if your domain requires.[Eval: Recall@10 uplift ≥ +6pp?] | Yes ------------------> [Keep current template] | |[Strengthen rewrite: add "overview/history/timeline" phrasing] |[Still low uplift? Add domain anchors or reduce generalization] Maximise context with minimal noise, then present coherent passages.Dense search top-k ≈ 30 (semantic).Keyword/BM25 top-k ≈ 30 (precise tokens, IDs). (≈1.5–2.5k chars) to give the generator a readable context.Use 10–15% overlap to avoid boundary cuts. Apply only if they are hard requirements in the user Q (e.g., compliance, SKU versions).Cross-rerank (re-rank top 50–100): 40–180 msOverlap between dense and BM25 results (unique hits on each side).Reranker movements (how many relevant docs moved into top-k).Parent dedup ratio (child→parent coalescing).[Corpus contains many codes/IDs/versions?] | Yes ------------------> [Use HYBRID + rerank] | |[Start vector-only; monitor misses w/ rare tokens → add BM25 if needed][Generator struggling (low EM/F1) despite decent Recall@10?] | Yes ------------------> [Lower k (8–10), increase parent coherence] | |[If recall is low, raise k to 12–15; keep rerank] Answer the question precisely, using only the retrieved evidence. “Answer strictly from provided documents; if insufficient, say so.”Original user question (with dates/IDs intact).List of k parent-level passages.Optional: ask the model to spans (doc title/ID + line or section). Deterministic, short first sentence with the fact; then 1–2 lines of support if needed.If evidence is missing/contradictory, return “not enough information” or request a clarifying filter.Red-flag unsupported claims (penalise in scoring).Latency target: 300–900 ms depending on model/context size.[High hallucination rate or compliance-critical domain?] | Yes ------------------> [Force extractive mode + citations; allow "I don't know"] | |[Allow light abstraction; still require references when possible]Failure Handling & Fallbacks with expanded synonyms; then merge. (e.g., timeframe, product version, entity disambiguation). (“Did you mean the 2016 season or total tenure?”). Return partial results (“Top 5 found; re-run to refine by year”).[Top-k contains 0–2 relevant docs] | Yes ------------------> [Relax filters → expand k → lighten rewrite → hybrid on] | |5) Observability, Metrics, and Business Tie-In Recall@k (+6–15pp target), Precision@k, unique hits by method (dense vs BM25), reranker lift (nDCG gain). EM/F1 (+3–10pp target), hallucination rate (−20–40% relative), citation coverage. Rewrite + retrieval + rerank + generation within your SLA (e.g., ≤1.5–2.0s p95). Rewrite adds one LLM call — keep it ≤10–20% of per-query spend via caching. fewer back-and-forths to find the right slides/SKUs/policies. date/version-bound answers with sources.Rollout Plan (Safe & Incremental) Log rewrites and top-k hits without changing user output; verify gains offline. Enable step-back for 50% traffic; monitor Recall@10, EM/F1, latency, cost. Adjust rewrite strength; enable hybrid+rerrank if rare-token misses persist. Turn on extractive mode + citations for regulated flows Cache frequent rewrites and merged top-k for hot intents; revisit budgets quarterly.[Targets met: Recall@10 +6–15pp; EM/F1 +3–10pp; Hallucinations ↓20–40%; SLA met] | Yes ------------------> [Roll out + monitor drift/costs/CSAT] | |[Retune rewrite strength → add/weight hybrid → refine reranker → re-test]This flow adds (the rewrite) that consistently expands the retrieval target, then tightens precision at the generation stage. With hybrid search, reranking, parent-level context, and clear guardrails, you can deliver in accuracy and reliability — while staying inside budgets that make sense for the business.Prompt Templates (Zero-shot & Few-shot)Use this when you want minimal overhead:You are a world-knowledge assistant.Rewrite the user’s question into a more general “step-back” questionthat is easier to answer and more comprehensive for retrieval.Return only the rewritten question.Few-shot (Recommended for Consistency)Add a couple of examples to anchor behaviour:You are an expert at world knowledge. Rewrite the input question intoa more generic “step-back” question that broadens scope for retrieval."input": "Could the members of The Police perform lawful arrests?""output": "What can the members of The Police do?""input": "Jan Šindel was born in what country?""output": "What is Jan Šindel’s personal history?"Now rewrite the next input accordingly. Return only the rewritten question.Keep outputs short, noun-phrase friendly, and time-agnostic unless time is core.Avoid adding claims; you’re changing the query granularity.Minimal Implementation (Python)Below is a concise, production-friendly skeleton. Swap embed(), vector_search(), and chat() with your stack (OpenAI, Cohere, Vertex, etc.). The retrieval can be dense, keyword, or .# core.pyfrom typing import List, DictSTEPBACK_SYSTEM = """You are an expert at world knowledge.Rewrite the input question into a broader 'step-back' question thatcaptures general context for retrieval. Return only the rewritten question.Examples:input: Could the members of The Police perform lawful arrests?output: What can the members of The Police do?input: Jan Šindel was born in what country?output: What is Jan Šindel’s personal history?def chat(messages: List[Dict[str, str]]) -> str: # Implement with your LLM client; return the assistant's text.def embed(texts: List[str]) -> List[List[float]]: # Implement with your embedding client; return list of vectors.def vector_search(query_vec, k=8) -> List[Dict]: # Implement with your vector DB (e.g., FAISS, pgvector, Pinecone). # Return: [{"text": "...chunk...", "score": 0.87}, ...] ...def hybrid_search(query: str, k=8) -> List[Dict]: # Optional: combine keyword (BM25) + vector results, then rerank.def generate_stepback(user_q: str) -> str: return chat([ {"role": "system", "content": STEPBACK_SYSTEM}, {"role": "user", "content": user_q},def retrieve(stepback_q: str, k: int = 6, mode: str = "vector") -> List[str]: if mode == "hybrid": hits = hybrid_search(stepback_q, k=k) else: qv = embed([stepback_q])[0] hits = vector_search(qv, k=k) return [h["text"] for h in hits]GENERATOR_SYSTEM = "Answer strictly using the provided documents. If uncertain, say so."def generate_answer(original_q: str, docs: List[str]) -> str: prompt = f"""Use ONLY the documents below to answer the question.{docs}""" {"role": "system", "content": GENERATOR_SYSTEM}, {"role": "user", "content": prompt}, ])def rag_with_stepback(question: str, k: int = 6, mode: str = "vector") -> str: stepback_q = generate_stepback(question) docs = retrieve(stepback_q, k=k, mode=mode) return generate_answer(question, docs)Evaluating the Gains (Recall, Precision, MRR)Track retrieval answer quality before/after adding step-back:: % of queries where a gold-supporting passage appears in top-k.: % of retrieved passages that are actually useful.: Rank-aware quality.Answer EM / F1 / BLEU / ROUGE: Task-level metrics, depending on domain.: % of answers unsupported by retrieved evidence.: Rewriting + broader retrieval can add a few hundred ms — measure it.Baseline RAG (no rewriting)RAG + step-back rewriting Compare metrics across a held-out eval set (50–500 queries is usually enough for directional results).Simple Accuracy Optimisation StrategiesPrecision-First System Constants// Accuracy-optimized bounds (trading speed for correctness)// 2x normal for complex queriesDEEP_SEMANTIC_ANALYSIS_CANDIDATES := 128 // extensive evidence for reasoning MULTI_HOP_REASONING_EVIDENCE_LIMIT := 256// thorough cross-validationCROSS_VALIDATION_RERANK_CANDIDATES := 64// rich contextual understandingHIERARCHICAL_CONTEXT_ASSEMBLY_LIMIT := 32// multi-step logical reasoningITERATIVE_REASONING_CHAIN_LIMIT := 8// Understanding depth parameters// deep query understandingSEMANTIC_DECOMPOSITION_DEPTH := 5// multi-hop concept linkingCONCEPTUAL_RELATIONSHIP_HOPS := 4// iterative evidence synthesisEVIDENCE_SYNTHESIS_ITERATIONS := 3// comprehensive validationLOGICAL_CONSISTENCY_CHECKS := 4// Complex task handling thresholds// identify complex reasoning tasksCONCEPTUAL_COMPLEXITY_THRESHOLD := 0.90// cross-domain knowledge synthesisMULTI_DOMAIN_INTEGRATION_THRESHOLD := 0.85// high bar for causal claimsCAUSAL_REASONING_CONFIDENCE_MINIMUM := 0.95// strict temporal accuracyTEMPORAL_REASONING_PRECISION := 0.98function execute_maximum_accuracy_reasoning_pipeline( complex_user_query: immutable_string) -> comprehensive_reasoning_result: // Phase 1: Deep Query Understanding and Decomposition query_complexity_profile := analyze_query_complexity_dimensions(complex_user_query) if query_complexity_profile.complexity_score >= CONCEPTUAL_COMPLEXITY_THRESHOLD: decomposed_subqueries := perform_hierarchical_query_decomposition( complex_user_query, SEMANTIC_DECOMPOSITION_DEPTH ) reasoning_strategy := select_complex_reasoning_strategy(query_complexity_profile) else: decomposed_subqueries := [complex_user_query] reasoning_strategy := "direct_evidence_synthesis" // Phase 2: Multi-Perspective Evidence Gathering comprehensive_evidence_base := gather_multi_perspective_evidence( decomposed_subqueries, query_complexity_profile ) // Phase 3: Deep Conceptual Understanding conceptual_relationship_graph := construct_conceptual_relationship_graph( comprehensive_evidence_base, CONCEPTUAL_RELATIONSHIP_HOPS ) domain_expertise_context := synthesize_domain_specific_expertise( conceptual_relationship_graph, query_complexity_profile // Phase 4: Iterative Evidence Synthesis and Reasoning synthesized_understanding := perform_iterative_evidence_synthesis( comprehensive_evidence_base, conceptual_relationship_graph, domain_expertise_context, EVIDENCE_SYNTHESIS_ITERATIONS // Phase 5: Multi-Step Logical Reasoning if reasoning_strategy == "multi_hop_logical_reasoning": reasoning_chain := construct_logical_reasoning_chain( synthesized_understanding, ITERATIVE_REASONING_CHAIN_LIMIT validated_reasoning := validate_logical_consistency( reasoning_chain, LOGICAL_CONSISTENCY_CHECKS ) validated_reasoning := synthesized_understanding // Phase 6: Comprehensive Answer Generation with Uncertainty Quantification final_answer := generate_comprehensive_answer_with_uncertainty( complex_user_query, validated_reasoning, query_complexity_profile ) // Phase 7: Multi-Dimensional Quality Assurance quality_assessment := perform_comprehensive_quality_assessment( final_answer, validated_reasoning, query_complexity_profile ) if quality_assessment.meets_accuracy_standards: return create_high_confidence_result(final_answer, quality_assessment) return perform_accuracy_recovery_procedure( complex_user_query, validated_reasoning, quality_assessment )Query Understanding and Analysisfunction analyze_query_complexity_dimensions( user_query: immutable_string) -> query_complexity_profile: // Semantic complexity analysis semantic_depth := analyze_semantic_depth_requirements(user_query) conceptual_abstractness := measure_conceptual_abstraction_level(user_query) domain_interdependency := detect_multi_domain_knowledge_requirements(user_query) // Reasoning complexity analysis logical_reasoning_type := identify_required_reasoning_patterns(user_query) causal_relationship_complexity := analyze_causal_reasoning_requirements(user_query) temporal_reasoning_demands := assess_temporal_reasoning_complexity(user_query) // Knowledge integration complexity cross_domain_synthesis_needs := detect_cross_domain_synthesis_requirements(user_query) evidence_integration_difficulty := estimate_evidence_synthesis_complexity(user_query) uncertainty_quantification_needs := assess_uncertainty_handling_requirements(user_query) // Compute overall complexity score complexity_score := compute_weighted_complexity_score( semantic_depth, conceptual_abstractness, domain_interdependency, logical_reasoning_type, causal_relationship_complexity, temporal_reasoning_demands, cross_domain_synthesis_needs, evidence_integration_difficulty, uncertainty_quantification_needs ) return create_complexity_profile( complexity_score, logical_reasoning_type, domain_interdependency, causal_relationship_complexity, temporal_reasoning_demands, cross_domain_synthesis_needs, evidence_integration_difficultyfunction perform_hierarchical_query_decomposition( complex_query: immutable_string, decomposition_depth: immutable_integer) -> hierarchical_subquery_structure: // Level 1: Core question identification core_question_components := extract_fundamental_question_components(complex_query) // Level 2: Prerequisite knowledge identification prerequisite_concepts := identify_prerequisite_conceptual_knowledge(core_question_components) // Level 3: Dependency chain analysis knowledge_dependency_graph := construct_knowledge_dependency_graph( core_question_components, prerequisite_concepts ) // Level 4: Atomic query generation atomic_subqueries := generate_atomic_knowledge_queries(knowledge_dependency_graph) // Level 5: Logical ordering and prioritization ordered_query_sequence := determine_optimal_query_execution_order( atomic_subqueries, knowledge_dependency_graph ) return create_hierarchical_structure( core_question_components, prerequisite_concepts, knowledge_dependency_graph, atomic_subqueries, ordered_query_sequence )Multi-Perspective Evidence Gathering Analysisfunction gather_multi_perspective_evidence( decomposed_queries: immutable_hierarchical_subquery_structure, complexity_profile: immutable_query_complexity_profile) -> comprehensive_evidence_base: multi_modal_evidence := create_empty_evidence_collection() for each atomic_query in decomposed_queries.ordered_query_sequence: // Deep semantic retrieval with expanded search space deep_semantic_evidence := retrieve_deep_semantic_evidence( atomic_query, DEEP_SEMANTIC_ANALYSIS_CANDIDATES ) // Conceptual relationship evidence conceptual_evidence := retrieve_conceptual_relationship_evidence( atomic_query, complexity_profile.domain_interdependency ) // Cross-domain knowledge synthesis evidence if complexity_profile.cross_domain_synthesis_needs > 0.7: cross_domain_evidence := retrieve_cross_domain_synthesis_evidence( atomic_query, complexity_profile.domain_interdependency multi_modal_evidence := integrate_evidence( multi_modal_evidence, cross_domain_evidence ) // Temporal and causal evidence for complex reasoning if complexity_profile.causal_relationship_complexity > 0.8: causal_evidence := retrieve_causal_relationship_evidence( atomic_query, complexity_profile.temporal_reasoning_demands multi_modal_evidence := integrate_evidence( multi_modal_evidence, causal_evidence ) // Integrate all evidence modalities multi_modal_evidence := integrate_evidence( multi_modal_evidence, deep_semantic_evidence, conceptual_evidence ) // Perform cross-query evidence validation and synthesis validated_evidence_base := perform_cross_query_evidence_validation( multi_modal_evidence, decomposed_queries.knowledge_dependency_graph ) return validated_evidence_basefunction retrieve_deep_semantic_evidence( atomic_query: immutable_string, candidate_limit: immutable_integer) -> deep_semantic_evidence_set: // Multi-granularity semantic search sentence_level_evidence := semantic_search_at_granularity( atomic_query, "sentence", candidate_limit / 4 ) paragraph_level_evidence := semantic_search_at_granularity( atomic_query, "paragraph", candidate_limit / 4 section_level_evidence := semantic_search_at_granularity( atomic_query, "section", candidate_limit / 4 document_level_evidence := semantic_search_at_granularity( atomic_query, "document", candidate_limit / 4 // Semantic coherence validation across granularities coherence_validated_evidence := validate_cross_granularity_coherence( sentence_level_evidence, paragraph_level_evidence, section_level_evidence, document_level_evidence // Deep reranking with conceptual understanding conceptually_reranked_evidence := perform_conceptual_understanding_reranking( coherence_validated_evidence, atomic_query ) return conceptually_reranked_evidenceConceptual Relationship Graph Constructionfunction construct_conceptual_relationship_graph( evidence_base: immutable_comprehensive_evidence_base, relationship_hops: immutable_integer) -> conceptual_relationship_graph: // Extract core concepts from evidence core_concepts := extract_domain_concepts_with_confidence( evidence_base, minimum_confidence := 0.90 ) // Build concept-to-concept relationship matrix concept_relationship_matrix := initialize_relationship_matrix(core_concepts) for each concept_pair in all_pairs(core_concepts): relationship_strength := compute_conceptual_relationship_strength( concept_pair, evidence_base ) relationship_type := classify_relationship_type( concept_pair, evidence_base concept_relationship_matrix := update_relationship( concept_relationship_matrix, concept_pair, relationship_strength, relationship_type ) // Construct multi-hop reasoning paths reasoning_paths := construct_multi_hop_reasoning_paths( concept_relationship_matrix, relationship_hops ) // Validate logical consistency of reasoning paths logically_consistent_paths := validate_reasoning_path_consistency( reasoning_paths, evidence_base ) return create_conceptual_graph( core_concepts, concept_relationship_matrix, logically_consistent_pathsfunction synthesize_domain_specific_expertise( conceptual_graph: immutable_conceptual_relationship_graph, complexity_profile: immutable_query_complexity_profile) -> domain_expertise_context: // Identify relevant domain expertise areas required_expertise_domains := identify_expertise_requirements( conceptual_graph, complexity_profile ) domain_specific_contexts := create_empty_context_collection() for each expertise_domain in required_expertise_domains: // Gather domain-specific methodological knowledge methodological_context := retrieve_domain_methodologies( expertise_domain, conceptual_graph ) // Gather domain-specific validation criteria validation_criteria := retrieve_domain_validation_standards( expertise_domain, complexity_profile ) // Gather domain-specific uncertainty quantification approaches uncertainty_quantification := retrieve_domain_uncertainty_methods( expertise_domain, complexity_profile ) domain_context := create_domain_context( expertise_domain, methodological_context, validation_criteria, uncertainty_quantification ) domain_specific_contexts := add_domain_context( domain_specific_contexts, domain_context return integrate_domain_contexts(domain_specific_contexts)Iterative Evidence Synthesis with Reasoningfunction perform_iterative_evidence_synthesis( evidence_base: immutable_comprehensive_evidence_base, conceptual_graph: immutable_conceptual_relationship_graph, domain_context: immutable_domain_expertise_context, synthesis_iterations: immutable_integer) -> synthesized_understanding: current_understanding := initialize_understanding_from_evidence(evidence_base) for iteration in range(synthesis_iterations): // Identify gaps in current understanding understanding_gaps := identify_knowledge_gaps( current_understanding, conceptual_graph ) // Synthesize evidence to fill identified gaps gap_filling_synthesis := synthesize_evidence_for_gaps( understanding_gaps, evidence_base, conceptual_graph ) // Validate synthesis against domain expertise domain_validated_synthesis := validate_against_domain_expertise( gap_filling_synthesis, domain_context ) // Update understanding with validated synthesis updated_understanding := integrate_validated_synthesis( current_understanding, domain_validated_synthesis ) // Check for convergence in understanding quality understanding_quality := assess_understanding_completeness( updated_understanding, conceptual_graph ) if understanding_quality.convergence_achieved: break current_understanding := updated_understanding return current_understandingfunction construct_logical_reasoning_chain( synthesized_understanding: immutable_synthesized_understanding, chain_length_limit: immutable_integer) -> logical_reasoning_chain: reasoning_premises := extract_logical_premises(synthesized_understanding) reasoning_chain := initialize_empty_reasoning_chain() for step in range(chain_length_limit): // Apply logical inference rules inference_candidates := apply_logical_inference_rules( reasoning_premises, synthesized_understanding ) // Validate inference soundness sound_inferences := validate_inference_soundness( inference_candidates, synthesized_understanding ) if empty(sound_inferences): break // Select strongest inference strongest_inference := select_highest_confidence_inference(sound_inferences) reasoning_chain := append_reasoning_step(reasoning_chain, strongest_inference) # Update premises with new inference reasoning_premises := update_premises_with_inference( reasoning_premises, strongest_inference )Comprehensive Answer Generation with Uncertaintyfunction generate_comprehensive_answer_with_uncertainty( original_query: immutable_string, validated_reasoning: immutable_logical_reasoning_chain, complexity_profile: immutable_query_complexity_profile) -> comprehensive_answer_with_uncertainty: // Generate core answer from reasoning chain core_answer := generate_core_answer_from_reasoning( original_query, validated_reasoning ) // Quantify epistemic uncertainty epistemic_uncertainty := quantify_epistemic_uncertainty( validated_reasoning, complexity_profile ) // Quantify aleatoric uncertainty aleatoric_uncertainty := quantify_aleatoric_uncertainty( validated_reasoning, complexity_profile ) // Generate confidence intervals for quantitative claims confidence_intervals := generate_confidence_intervals_for_claims( core_answer, validated_reasoning ) // Identify and articulate assumptions explicit_assumptions := identify_and_articulate_assumptions( validated_reasoning, complexity_profile ) // Generate alternative interpretations if uncertainty is high if epistemic_uncertainty.total_uncertainty > 0.3: alternative_interpretations := generate_alternative_interpretations( original_query, validated_reasoning, epistemic_uncertainty else: alternative_interpretations := [] return create_comprehensive_answer( core_answer, epistemic_uncertainty, aleatoric_uncertainty, confidence_intervals, explicit_assumptions, alternative_interpretations )Multi-Dimensional Quality Assurancefunction perform_comprehensive_quality_assessment( answer: immutable_comprehensive_answer_with_uncertainty, reasoning: immutable_logical_reasoning_chain, complexity_profile: immutable_query_complexity_profile)
